Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard VCS and HTTP sources pointing to Astral's official GitHub repository and release assets. The `source` array fetches a LICENSE file and platform-specific tarballs directly from the project's GitHub releases using HTTPS URLs. All checksums are provided and non-SKIP, ensuring integrity. The global scope contains only variable assignments and array definitions, with no command substitution, immediate function calls, or execution of external code. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no dynamic version resolution occurs during sourcing.

Fetching sources via `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will only clone or download from trusted upstream domains (github.com, raw.githubusercontent.com) under the astral-sh organization, which is consistent with standard AUR packaging practices. No `prepare()`, `build()`, or `package()` logic is executed under these flags, and the top-level code poses no risk of data exfiltration or remote code execution during source acquisition.

The `package()` function, while invoking the binary to generate shell completions, is not run during this phase and thus out of scope for this narrow gate. Standard build/install operations in later stages are expected and not a concern here.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe source fetch and parse[/]

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard MIT License declaration, containing only the expected copyright notice and permission wording. There is no executable code, network activity, file manipulation, or any form of obfuscated or suspicious content. The mention of "Astral Software Inc." and the year 2025 are part of the license header and do not indicate malicious intent. License files in AUR packages are common and expected, and this one shows no signs of tampering or hidden payloads.

The content is purely legal text and poses no security risk. It does not perform any actions, request external resources, or modify the system. Such files are inert and used solely for compliance and attribution purposes.
</details>
<summary>
Standard MIT license, no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed LICENSE-0.0.51. Status: SAFE -- Standard MIT license, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves prebuilt binaries from the official Astral-sh/ty GitHub repository releases, matching the declared upstream source. The URLs use HTTPS and point directly to versioned release assets, with checksums provided and verified for all architectures. The license file is also fetched via HTTPS from the corresponding Git tag. There is no code compilation or execution of downloaded scripts, and the binary is installed as-is into the standard path.

The package generates shell completions by running the downloaded binary's subcommands (`generate-shell-completion`) and piping output into the appropriate directories. This is a safe and common practice for tools that support it, as it uses the legitimate functionality of the upstream binary in a predictable way. No network requests, file system modifications outside `$pkgdir`, or system changes occur during packaging.

All operations align with standard AUR packaging practices. The use of `!strip` is justified for a prebuilt binary where stripping symbols might remove needed metadata. No obfuscated code, suspicious commands, or data exfiltration risks are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard, safe AUR packaging of upstream binary</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for README.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `README.md` file contains documentation for the `ty-bin` AUR package, explaining its purpose, installation instructions, and compatibility details. It does not contain executable code or any malicious content. The file describes a legitimate use case—replacing the official Arch `ty` package on systems with 16 KiB memory pages due to jemalloc incompatibility—and clarifies that the package uses upstream binaries from Astral's GitHub Releases, which are verifiable via published checksums and GitHub Attestations.

There are no signs of obfuscation, unauthorized network operations, data exfiltration, or supply-chain attacks. The instructions to use helpers like `paru` or `yay` are standard for AUR packages. References to verification commands like `gh attestation verify` promote security best practices rather than posing a risk. All links point to the official upstream project, and the described behavior aligns with normal AUR packaging workflows.

The content solely serves an informational role and poses no security threat.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation-only file; no executable code or malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines variables and source arrays in its global scope. All source URLs point to the official upstream GitHub repository (astral-sh/ty) and use HTTPS. Checksums are provided for all source files. There is no `pkgver()` function, and no top-level command substitutions or dangerous operations (e.g., `eval`, `curl|bash`, base64 decoding) that would execute during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The `package()` function is not executed by these commands, so its content is out of scope for this gate. Fetching the declared sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code in global scope; sources are from official upstream.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD is a straightforward prebuilt binary package for the `ty` Python type checker. It downloads tarballs from the official GitHub releases page (`https://github.com/astral-sh/ty/releases/download/...`), pins each architecture with a SHA-256 checksum, installs the binary into `/usr/bin`, and generates shell completions by invoking the installed binary. There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests (aside from fetching the package's own upstream source), and no dangerous command execution. Running the upstream binary to generate completion files is standard practice for such packages. All operations are limited to the package's own scope. No genuine supply-chain risk is present.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR bin package with checksums, no suspicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

There is no executable code, obfuscation, network requests, or any system manipulation. All content is descriptive and standard for an AUR package description. The references to AUR helpers (paru/yay) and upstream URLs are expected and benign.
